Q32: I have extensive and intimate knowledge of CA nearshore ecosystems. I studied Marine Biology at UCSC and continue to work in the field of marine science. I also actively participate in our fisheries as well as numerous other ocean based activities. The ocean is my life and I am especially concerned with what appears to be a widening gap between those who manage our ocean resources and those who spend the most time in, on or around the ocean. I believe CA leads the country in terms of marine resource management, but I worry that the ongoing MPA petitions and 30x30 process go too far. MPAs are not the appropriate tool for and may not be effective at mitigating many of the current and future risks our marine resources are facing.

Q32: In Marin County around Tomales Bay, public land and water are managed by myriad agencies from the county, state and federal governement. These entities interact and collaborate poorly, allowing continued damage to tomales bay by ignorant fishermen, ignorant boat-in campers, ignorant car-based tourists. County park lands are used to access sanctuary waters and NPS lands for camping. County parks does not staff their areas high enough to manage trash brought by all of the above user groups. County Park's solution is to periodically bring in CCCNB staff to pickup trash, when, they should have more staff on hand regularly to do in-person outreach and education. The NPS provides many small beaches for camping on the west shore, yet does very little patrolling to ensure people do not use the bushes as toilets, or leave trash, build permanent structures and fire rings on beaches marked as “NO CAMPIN”. The USCG rarely is present at boat launches to ensure safety equipment is being used by boaters. Reactive rescues are the norm, as opposed to more proactive, person-to-person education to teach people safe boating before they get in trouble. Putting up signs is not working. People don't read them much. There needs to be MUCH BETTER collaboration amongst the various agencies in order to protect nature, and ensure people are safe.

Q32: I am downright scared at the direction that “Marine resource management “ has taken over the last 20 years.   It feels like marine resource management has become more of an anti fishing agenda.   As a fishermen we love the ocean and all that it offers and want to conserve it for future generations of fishermen.  I feel like SPORT/Recreational fisherman are under attack for enjoying the resource.  There are vast areas that are already sanctuaries and I feel like “they/those in the rule making process, mostly environmentalists that enjoy looking at the ocean,  want to get us off of the ocean from fishing.  There are rules, regulations, sanctuaries, etc in place to help fish thrive and allow us to enjoy the ocean and its resources in a reasonable and responsible manner without completely closing access to them.   Closing areas completely, such as Cordell Bank for example simply pushes more boats into smaller areas to target fish.  Closing access to ocean areas for wind farms harms our enjoyment of the ocean access and ability to catch fish where they are.   With the creation of wind farms how many birds will be killed, windmill fires that will pollute ocean happen, while we will not be able to fish.  I am extremely concerned about the 30 and 30 executive order that Gavin Newsome enacted. We already have MPA and marine sanctuaries.

Q32: Our marine life is healthy. After a long history of severe overfishing, the last 30 years have been a remarkable turnaround. We have almost no fish or invertebrates that are overfished, subject to overfishing or endangered. By far the biggest threat is NGOs and special interest groups that have an awful anti-fishing stance and threaten constantly to take away more and more of our ocean. And we rightfully lack faith in the government to meaningfully and objectively determine if there’s the need to close more ocean areas. We are fully justified in believing that these anti-fishing groups have all the pull and we have none. Our fishery is booming but we feel it is doomed to being closed off entirely. The closures being proposed now are not just unnecessary, they’re completely unethical and based on a prima facie bogus concept, as it is nonsense to say fishing contributed to the loss in kelp. We have always as a group obeyed limits and cared about being good stewards of the ocean recourses, but despite our great behavior and rule following for all this years we continually get threatened with more and more closures. Given the state of our fishery, we can only conclude the governments agenda is to end fishing. To create a “look-but-don’t-touch” relationship with nature. Fishing brought my stepdad into my life. Gave me lifelong friends and some of my best memories. Is by far the most important thing for my mental health. Yet I worry. Every year it seems like some new attack, from the new petitions to 30x30 to the salmon closures to the indefensible changes in rockfish seasons and limits. And these closures always target the best habitat and safest areas to fish and dive. It’s very hard to have any faith in the system when you see it so clearly rigged against you. It’s incredibly sad and the people behind it are so misguided. I wish I could take them all out fishing just once, so they could see how evil what they’re doing really is. There’s a difference between being an environmentalist and being a protectionist. The former can be a great thing, the latter is horrible.

Q32: Hello, I have considered myself a waterman since I was 16. I have been an ocean lifeguard for the state of California and an active freedive spearfisher using the game I catch to feed my family and community. This respect for the ocean led me to become an aviation rescue swimmer in the navy where my respect and knowledge of the ocean was furthered. Currently I am a firefighter and help teach our marine fire fighting class. My number one concern is the health of our ocean as it is directly tied to my ability to feed my family. Additionally, free diving and spearfishing are what I use to keep my mental health in a good place. I do not feel represented in the decisions currently being made. This is made worse when the president of the fish and game commission Samantha Murray make comments about how fishing is a privilege not a right, unaware the fishing is a right in California. We all want the same thing, a future with a healthy ocean, it seems absurd to not listen to the voices of the people who spend the majority of their free time in the ocean. Thank you

Q32: I believe that the term commercial fishing is used very loosely. There are significant differences in sustainability between rod and reel caught fish, versus long lining, or purse seining. Not to mention the difference in survivability of bycatch. Large scale commercial fishing is also significantly different than a small time, hook and line commercial fisherman. Lumping both of these practices together is fairly irresponsible for those that know these major differences yet still combine them while approaching the fairly unaware public eye. Groups like Coastal Conservation Association have done a lot of good, both in representing the small time fisherman, as well as the local marine ecosystems that are being used. I believe in some instances preservation is necessary but it limits an individual from truly being a part of their environment the way that conversation allows. They can only be a brief visitor in that scenario. A healthy, clean caught fish that can nourish my family is only able to be caught again through stewardship and care for that same ecosystem. That conservationist being a part of that ecosystem directly benefits from the ecosystem flourishing far more than the individual experiencing it through preservation. Preservation puts the individual as “outside” or alien to that ecosystem which inhibits true connection in my opinion. Conservation allows individuals to feel more of an obligation to sustain a healthy environment far more than preservation allows. I wish California could develop the kind of trust between fishermen and the Fish and Wildlife state service that Maine had to have in order to save their lobster fishery. It’s highly monitored but ultimately they worked together to rebuild the fishery. Here it feels like there is a growing mistrust which I do not see as a good direction. Once communication and trust go away, a very toxic situation will be all that’s left.

Q32: I am a long time resident goleta. I'm working class person who has raised a family here and has as a sustenance Coastal user fed my family from our Coast. I do spearfishing and some bleach coming slash Gathering and foraging. For many years I was able to Freed my family fish and shellfish that we could never afford to buy primarily from devero/coil oil point which is now closed to all sustenance users for the first time in 13,000 years. We were promised adaptive management when the Marine protected areas were put in and all we've seen is closures with nothing ever reopening, no compromise or adaptive management that I can see. I feel like for people who are not using a boat and are going from the shore using traditional methods such as hook and line and spearfishing that there should be a report card or some other form of adaptive management that allows us to add a human element to Marine particular areas that belong there. Additionally things like having the commercial size limit for lobsters be the same as a recreational limit for free diving seems inherently unfair. I strongly believe that restrictions on Commercial Seafood particularly for export and not for local community consumption should be restricted and there should be higher threshold for taking resources for the ocean really for profit and export as opposed to helping feed our local community. Appreciate your guys effort together this material how many UC Santa Cruz alumni myself.
